Why is it still so hard for humanity to love in a holistic way?
We can split atoms, build AI, send rockets into space — yet we still struggle with ego, fear, revenge and power. Technologically advanced, but emotionally primitive.
This is not against any people — not Israelis, not Iranians, not Americans, not anyone. Governments make decisions. Civilians carry the consequences.
If a book is called holy, then act holy.
If faith needs force, it is no longer faith.
If justice requires bombs, it stops being justice.
To the Iranian government: you cannot repress your way into genuine belief. If your interpretation is true, allow debate. Release peaceful dissenters. Compulsion is not faith.
To students and protesters: stay disciplined. Do not let legitimate frustration turn into hatred. Moral clarity is your strength.
To external powers: de-escalate and protect civilians. Bombs rarely create reform — they often strengthen the hardest voices inside a country.
To civilians everywhere: refuse dehumanization. The moment we reduce entire populations to enemies, we prepare the ground for more graves.
And to religious leaders everywhere:
The Qur’an says, “There is no compulsion in religion” (2:256).
“If your Lord had willed, all would have believed — will you then compel them?” (10:99).
“Whoever kills a soul… it is as if he killed all mankind” (5:32).
“Stand firmly for justice, even against yourselves” (4:135).
“Repel evil with what is better” (41:34).
If these verses are holy, then act in accordance with them.
Faith does not need force.
Holiness does not need missiles.
Truth does not fear debate.
We are one blood — students, soldiers, refugees, mothers, children.
Love is not weakness. Love is disciplined restraint. Love is refusing to mirror the violence you oppose.
Otherwise the words “love” and “holy” become empty.
When the ocean of compassion runs dry, no one can fish for peace.
